result.markdown:
--- title: "Quarto Notes" subtitle: This is for how to use Quarto to write notes editor_options: markdown: wrap: sentence canonical: true --- It is a good start to learn how to use `quarto` document. In general, the usage of `quarto` is very similar to `markdown`. ## Markdown Basic ### Syntax | Syntax | Output | Syntax | Output | |------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------| | `*italics*` | *italics* | `**bold**` | **bold** | | `***bold italics***` | ***bold italics*** | `~~strikethrough~~` | ~~strikethrough~~ | | `superscript^2^` | superscript^2^ | `subscript_2` | subscript_2 | | `` | | `[Quarto](https://quarto.org)` | [Quarto](https://quarto.org) | ### List +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | Markdown Syntax | Output | +=================================+=================================+ | ``` markdown | - unordered list | | * unordered list | - sub-item 1 | | + sub-item 1 | - sub-item 2 | | + sub-item 2 | - sub-sub-item 1 | | - sub-sub-item 1 | | | ``` | | +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | ``` markdown | - item 2 | | * item 2 | | | | Continued (indent 4 spaces) | | Continued (indent 4 spaces) | | | ``` | | +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | ``` markdown | 1. ordered list | | 1. ordered list | 2. item 2 | | 2. item 2 | i) sub-item 1 | | i) sub-item 1 | A. sub-sub-item 1 | | A. sub-sub-item 1 | | | ``` | | +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | ``` markdown | (1) A list whose numbering | | (@) A list whose numbering | | | | continues after | | continues after | | | | (2) an interruption | | (@) an interruption | | | ``` | | +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | ``` markdown |
| | ::: {} | | | 1. A list | 1. A list | | ::: | | | |
| | ::: {} | | | 1. Followed by another list |
| | ::: | | | ``` | 1. Followed by another list | | | | | |
| +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ | ``` markdown | term | | term | | | : definition | : definition | | ``` | | +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+ ## Code ::: callout-note To present the code without running it, you can either add " \`\`\`\` markdown " before the code and " `\`\`\`\` " after the code. Or you can use " \`\`\`{{markdown}} " ::: ### R ::: {.cell filename='R'} ```{.r .cell-code} 1 + 1 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2 ``` ::: ::: ### Python To install python packages under a specific version: ``` {.bash filename="Terminal"} python3.12 -m pip install pandas ``` Need to load `reticulate` package before running python code: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell filename='Python'} ```{.python .cell-code} 1 + 1 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 2 ``` ::: ::: ## Panel ```{{markdown}} ::: panel-tabset #### Jupyter #### Knitr #### SAS #### Other ::: ``` ::: panel-tabset #### Jupyter ```{{python}} #| label: fig-charts #| fig-cap: "Charts" #| fig-subcap: #| - "First" #| - "Second" #| layout-ncol: 2 import matplotlib.pyplot as plt plt.plot([1,23,2,4]) plt.show() plt.plot([8,65,23,90]) plt.show() ``` #### Knitr ```{{r}} #| label: fig-charts #| fig-cap: "Charts" #| fig-subcap: #| - "mpg" #| - "HP" #| layout-ncol: 2 plot(mtcars$cyl, mtcars$mpg) hist(mtcars$hp) ``` #### SAS #### Other ::: ## Table ### Table Example 1 ```{{markdown}} | Default | Left | Right | Center | |---------|:-----|------:|:------:| | 12 | 12 | 12 | 12 | | 123 | 123 | 123 | 123 | | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | : Demonstration of pipe table syntax ``` | Default | Left | Right | Center | |---------|:-----|------:|:------:| | 12 | 12 | 12 | 12 | | 123 | 123 | 123 | 123 | | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | : Demonstration of pipe table syntax ### Table Example 2 ```{{quarto}} ::: {#tbl-panel layout-ncol="2"} | Col1 | Col2 | Col3 | |------|------|------| | A | B | C | | E | F | G | | A | G | G | : First Table {#tbl-first} | Col1 | Col2 | Col3 | |------|------|------| | A | B | C | | E | F | G | | A | G | G | : Second Table {#tbl-second} Main Caption ::: See @tbl-panel for details, especially @tbl-second. ``` ::: {#tbl-panel layout-ncol="2"} | Col1 | Col2 | Col3 | |------|------|------| | A | B | C | | E | F | G | | A | G | G | : First Table {#tbl-first} | Col1 | Col2 | Col3 | |------|------|------| | A | B | C | | E | F | G | | A | G | G | : Second Table {#tbl-second} Main Caption ::: See @tbl-panel for details, especially @tbl-second. ### Table Example 3 ::: {#tbl-planets .cell filename='Python' tbl-cap='Astronomical object'} ```{.python .cell-code} # #| label: tbl-planets # #| tbl-cap: Astronomical object from IPython.display import Markdown from tabulate import tabulate table = [["Sun","696,000",1.989e30], ["Earth","6,371",5.972e24], ["Moon","1,737",7.34e22], ["Mars","3,390",6.39e23]] Markdown(tabulate( table, headers=["Astronomical object","R (km)", "mass (kg)"] )) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Astronomical object R (km) mass (kg) --------------------- -------- ----------- Sun 696,000 1.989e+30 Earth 6,371 5.972e+24 Moon 1,737 7.34e+22 Mars 3,390 6.39e+23 ::: ::: ### Grid Tables Grid tables are a more advanced type of markdown tables that allow arbitrary block elements (multiple paragraphs, code blocks, lists, etc.). For example: ``` markdown +-----------+-----------+--------------------+ | Fruit | Price | Advantages | +===========+===========+====================+ | Bananas | $1.34 | - built-in wrapper | | | | - bright color | +-----------+-----------+--------------------+ | Oranges | $2.10 | - cures scurvy | | | | - tasty | +-----------+-----------+--------------------+ : Sample grid table. ``` Which looks like this when rendered to HTML: +---------+--------+----------------------+ | Fruit | Price | Advantages | +=========+========+======================+ | Bananas | \$1.34 | - built-in wrapper | | | | - bright color | +---------+--------+----------------------+ | Oranges | \$2.10 | - cures scurvy | | | | - tasty | +---------+--------+----------------------+ : Sample grid table. The row of `=`s separates the header from the table body, and can be omitted for a headerless table. Cells that span multiple columns or rows are not supported. Alignments can be specified as with pipe tables, by putting colons at the boundaries of the separator line after the header: ``` markdown +---------+--------+------------------+ | Right | Left | Centered | +========:+:=======+:================:+ | Bananas | $1.34 | built-in wrapper | +---------+--------+------------------+ ``` Which looks like this when rendered to HTML: | Right | Left | Centered | |--------:|:-------|:----------------:| | Bananas | \$1.34 | built-in wrapper | For headerless tables, the colons go on the top line instead: ``` markdown +----------:+:----------+:--------:+ | Right | Left | Centered | +-----------+-----------+----------+ ``` Which looks like this when rendered to HTML: | | | | |------:|:-----|:--------:| | Right | Left | Centered | ## Figure To insert figure, you can use ``` markdown ![Elephant](elephant.png) ``` Examples to change width: ``` markdown ![Elephant](elephant.png){width=300} ![Elephant](elephant.png){width=80%} ![Elephant](elephant.png){width=4in} ``` To link a figure online, we can use ``` markdown [![Elephant](elephant.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elephant) ``` Additional functions: | Code | Functions | Example | |-------------|-----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------| | `fig-align` | figure alignment | `{fig-align="left"}` | | `fig-alt` | alternative text | `{fig-alt="A drawing of an elephant."}` | | `#fig-xxx` | cross references (use`@` for reference) | `{#fig-elephant}` | ::: callout-note For figures produced by executable code cells, include a `label` with a `fig-` prefix to make them cross-referenceable. For example: ```{{python}} #| label: fig-line-plot #| fig-cap: "A line plot " import matplotlib.pyplot as plt plt.plot([1,23,2,4]) plt.show() ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A line plot ](QuartoNotes_files/figure-html/fig-line-plot-1.png){#fig-line-plot width=614} ::: ::: For a demonstration of a line plot, see @fig-line-plot. #### Example of subfigures: ```{{markdown}} ::: {#fig-elephants layout-ncol=2 layout-nrow=2} ![Surus](surus.png){#fig-surus} ![Hanno](hanno.png){#fig-hanno} Famous Elephants ::: ``` ## Diagram ### Diagram Example 1 ```{{mermaid}} flowchart LR A[Hard edge] --> B(Round edge) B --> C{Decision} C --> D[Result one] C --> E[Result two] ``` ```{mermaid} flowchart LR A[Hard edge] --> B(Round edge) B --> C{Decision} C --> D[Result one] C --> E[Result two] ``` ## Divs and Spans You can add classes, attributes, and other identifiers to regions of content using Divs and Spans. For example, here we add the "border" class to a region of content using a div (`:::`): ``` markdown ::: {.border} This content can be styled with a border ::: ``` ::: border This content can be styled with a border ::: Once rendered to HTML, Quarto will translate the markdown into: ``` html

This content can be styled with a border

``` ## Callout Types There are five different types of callouts available. - `note`: `callout-note` - `warning`: `callout-warning` - `important`: `callout-important` - `tip`: `callout-tip` - `caution`: `callout-caution` The color and icon will be different depending upon the type that you select. Here are what the various types look like in HTML output: ::: callout-note Note that there are five types of callouts, including: `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `caution`, and `important`. ::: ::: callout-warning Callouts provide a simple way to attract attention, for example, to this warning. ::: ::: callout-important ## This is Important Danger, callouts will really improve your writing. ::: ::: callout-tip ## Tip With Title This is an example of a callout with a title. ::: ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"} ## Expand To Learn About Collapse This is an example of a 'collapsed' caution callout that can be expanded by the user. You can use `collapse="true"` to collapse it by default or `collapse="false"` to make a collapsible callout that is expanded by default. Code for this section: `{.callout-caution collapse="true"}` :::
